advantages: Hidden layers with nonlinear activations let MLPs fit curved and piecewise decision boundaries — solving problems like XOR that stump a single perceptron.
The feedforward MLP pattern is universal across frameworks (Linear → activation stacks) and easy to specify for tabular, embedding, and baseline image tasks.
Depth and hidden width provide explicit capacity knobs to trade underfitting against overfitting on a fixed dataset.
MLP blocks appear inside larger architectures (Transformer FFN), so fluency with hidden layers transfers directly to modern models.
disadvantages: Fully connected MLPs ignore spatial and sequential structure — they treat every input dimension independently, often needing more data and parameters than CNNs or RNNs on structured inputs.
Very deep MLPs without careful initialization or normalization can suffer vanishing or exploding activations, making training harder than shallow wide stacks for some tasks.
Parameter count grows with the product of adjacent layer widths — large hidden tiers become expensive in memory and compute compared to structured layers.
An MLP can approximate complex boundaries in theory but may overfit small datasets unless regularized (dropout, weight decay, early stopping).
